---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
#library(stargazer)
library(kableExtra)
library(rworldmap)
#library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)

# # Input data
data_2021 <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Project-SP23/Mirror_Class_Folder_Structure/Data/GII-2021-Tidy.xlsx", na = "NA")
# 
# # Test code for map
# data21_map <- joinCountryData2Map(dF = data_2021, nameJoinColumn = "iso3", joinCode = "ISO3")
# 
# mapCountryData(mapToPlot = data21_map, )

```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-data_blue_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, inverse

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size60[
Build the First Report

- Section 3: Univariate Analyses (Current)
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Make sure all data available on Canvas

2. Post Section 3 assignment details on Canvas

<br>

*Canvas*
1. Analyze the main index (`gii`) with a histogram (Fig 1) and a map (Fig 2)
2. Analyze at least two of the component measures with visualizations (Figs 3 and 4)


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size65[**Report 1: Submission Requirements**]

.size55[
- Submit a **pdf** to **Canvas** (due **Mar 5th**)

- Include an **APA** formatted bibliography

- Polish **all** included visualizations
]

???

Ok, this week we work on your first report.

- Here are the basic requirements.

- Late penalty (-10) points per day




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size50[**Report 1: Analyzing our Outcome Variable(s)**]

.size50[
1. What is it and why is this project important?

2. How confident should we be in the methodology?

3. What do the measures currently show us?

4. How are these measures changing across time?
]

???

1. In Section 1 your job is to give the reader a crash course in the chosen data project: What is it, who is behind it and why is it important?

2. Section 2 should step through the strengths and weaknesses of the measurements in the project.

3. Section 3 should focus on the most recent year available in the dataset and present a univariate analysis of it.

4. Section 4 should present univariate analyses at different snapshots in time so we can analyze the trends in the data

### Questions on the sections?

<br>

Plan of Attack:

- Today we'll make our visualizations for Section 3

- Wednesday we'll make our visualizations for Section 4.

- Friday we'll polish it all up




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07_1-Import_GII_2.png')
background-size: 90%
background-position: center
class: middle, slideblue

???

First thing's first, let's import the data.
- Seems like everyone prefers the RStudio menu approach

<br>

Make sure you:

1. Give the new object a clear name, and

2. If needed, identify how the data set records missing data.
    - Here you see the GII uses "NA" as a word to show missing data
    - With no argument in the NA field R assumes these variables are categorical and not numeric.
    - e.g. character vectors

### Make sense?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size70[**For Today**]

.size50[
1. Make separate histograms for the index (`gii`) and the five component measures (`mmr`, `abr`, `pr_f`, `se_f` and `lfpr_f`)

2. Make a map of `gii`<sup>*</sup>
]

.right[.size30[<sup>*</sup> Let me help with the map code...]]

???

**Don't polish, just get these seven visualizations built!**

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let me know when you get to the map step because we have to do this slightly differently.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size35[
```{r, echo=FALSE}
data_2021 |>
  select(iso3:gii) |>
  slice_head(n = 6) |>
  kbl(digits = 3) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 1, background = "orange")
```
]

.code130[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Join GII data to the map data
data_2021_map <- joinCountryData2Map(dF = data_2021, 
                                     joinCode = "ISO3", #<<
                                     nameJoinColumn = "iso3") #<<
```
]

???

The key step in making a map with rworldmap is when you have to merge your data onto the map data.

- The GII includes a special variable for this called "iso3"

<br>

Technically this is ISO 3166-1 which is a standard three letter country code published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), to represent countries, dependent territories, and special areas of geographical interest.

- Much more precise than asking rworldmap to guess based on country name.

<br>

If you're curious:

- rworldmap only understands 183 country names in our dataset, but

- it understands 195 of the ISO3 codes.

<br>

**SLIDE**: Ok, what should end up in your report?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size45[**Report 1: Analyzing our Outcome Variable(s)**]

<br>

.size40[**Section 3: What do the measures currently show us?**

1. Analyze the main index (`gii`) with a **histogram** (Fig 1) and a **map** (Fig 2)
    
2. Analyze **at least two** of the component measures with visualizations (Figs 3 and 4)
]

???

Here's how I think you should take today's work and use it in your report.

### Questions on this?

<br>

For Wednesday you should select and polish the four figures you will use in your report.
